high.claim: spec claims Linear two-way sync — webhook triggers builds AND state updates flow back to Linear
high.reality: webhook.py implements only the inbound direction: HMAC verification (verify_linear_signature), event parsing (parse_linear_event), and brief generation (linear_issue_to_brief). There are zero outbound Linear API calls in the provided source — no issueUpdate GraphQL mutation, no linear_update_issue helper, no HTTP POST to api.linear.app/graphql to transition issue state after a build completes or fails.
high.recommendation: Add an async helper (e.g. linear_update_issue_state) that POSTs the issueUpdate mutation to https://api.linear.app/graphql with Authorization: lin_api_... and call it from the build completion path in routes/sessions.py to close the two-way loop.

high.claim: spec marks test coverage as ✅ Complete (22-check smoke test suite)
high.reality: The test/ directory contains only unit tests for auth (test_auth.py) and webhook signature parsing (test_webhook.py). No integration or E2E tests exist covering the build pipeline: sessions.py route, the evaluator, or the push phase. scripts/smoke_test.py is a live-server HTTP probe that requires a running instance and cannot run in CI without one — it is not an integration test suite.
high.recommendation: Add pytest integration tests under tests/ that mock the Claude Agent SDK and exercise the full session-create → evaluator → phase-push flow without requiring a live server.

low.claim: spec marks test coverage as ✅ Complete (22-check smoke test suite)
low.reality: scripts/smoke_test.py itself carries the comment 'RA-1154: pipeline probe — canary marker, no functional change', indicating the pipeline check was added as a placeholder marker, not a real coverage assertion. The file is also truncated in the provided source so the 22-check count cannot be independently verified.
low.recommendation: Replace the canary-marker comment with a real pipeline invocation check (POST /api/build → poll /api/sessions/{id} until terminal state) and assert the 22-check count matches the actual number of passing assertions.
